critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-designed with several thoughtful controls. I'll focus on the genuine structural issues rather than manufacturing concerns.

**Issue 1: The annotated subset size relative to the split strategy creates a distributional mismatch problem**

The specific claim: Chronological splitting of 2,000–3,000 annotated reports into 0–24 months (train), 25–30 months (validation), and 31–36 months (test) will yield roughly proportional representation for all strata.

Why this might be wrong: With 2,000–3,000 total annotated reports spanning 36 months, stratified by modality AND department, the test set (6 of 36 months = ~17% of time) will contain roughly 340–510 reports. When further stratified by modality (4+) and department (3+), individual cells may contain 10–30 annotated instances. For rare modality-department combinations (e.g., MRI from interventional radiology), test cell sizes could fall below 10 spans per entity type. The macro-F1 across entity types within these small cells will have enormous variance, making the stratified comparisons (which the design emphasizes as evidence against template memorization) statistically unreliable. The headline macro-F1 may be nominally significant while the stratified controls that guard against confounds are individually underpowered.

What would constitute evidence: Report confidence intervals or bootstrap standard errors on each stratified F1. If the 95% CI on modality-specific F1 differences overlaps zero for any modality, the control is uninformative. Pre-register expected cell sizes per stratum and determine whether any stratum is below a minimum threshold before data collection.

---

**Issue 2: Pre-trained clinical transformer carries temporal leakage risk that the chronological split does not address**

The specific claim: Using a pre-trained clinical transformer (ClinicalBERT, BioBERT, etc.) fine-tuned on training reports is methodologically clean because the tokenizer is fit on training data only and fine-tuning uses only training labels.

Why this might be wrong: ClinicalBERT and similar models are pre-trained on corpora (e.g., MIMIC-III, PubMed) that may include reports or literature from overlapping time periods with the test set — and more importantly, may already encode entity patterns characteristic of the institution's reporting style if any of its data was in MIMIC or similar public releases. More concretely, the pre-training corpus for these models is fixed and public, but the institution's reports spanning 2021–2024 may share template language with data present in MIMIC (collected pre-2019 at a single institution) or share clinical vocabulary distributions with PubMed abstracts that post-date the training split. The CRF has no such pre-training advantage — its features are strictly derived from training data. This creates an asymmetric information advantage for the transformer that is not controlled for and cannot be eliminated by chronological splitting of the fine-tuning data.

The ablation proposed (context-window reduction) does not address this; it only tests architectural window size. There is no proposed ablation that isolates pre-training corpus contribution.

What would constitute evidence: Compare ClinicalBERT fine-tuned normally against (a) a transformer initialized from scratch (random weights) fine-tuned on the same training data, and (b) a general-domain BERT (not clinical) fine-tuned on the same data. If (a) performs near CRF and ClinicalBERT far exceeds it, the gain is from pre-training, not architecture. If ClinicalBERT still wins substantially over general BERT, the gain is from clinical pre-training specifically. Neither comparison is in the current design.

---

**Issue 3: Exact span match F1 as primary metric systematically disadvantages one model class in a way that is not clinically motivated**

The specific claim: Exact span match is the right operationalization of entity-level F1 because downstream pipelines require accurate, typed entity spans.

Why this might be wrong: CRF models with character-trigram and word-shape features tend to produce tighter, more conservative span boundaries, while fine-tuned transformers with subword tokenization can produce spans that include or exclude boundary tokens (determiners, prepositions) differently. A transformer predicting 'the right lower lobe' vs. the gold 'right lower lobe' counts as a false positive and false negative under exact match, even if the clinical content is identical. This asymmetry systematically penalizes whichever model class makes different but clinically equivalent boundary decisions — and there is no clinical argument that exact character-offset matching corresponds to downstream pipeline utility in a principled way. The methodology claims exact match aligns with operational goals but does not demonstrate this alignment.

What would constitute evidence: Report both exact-match F1 and a lenient-match F1 (e.g., any overlap with correct type counts as TP, or a head-token match). If transformer vs. CRF rankings reverse under lenient matching, the metric choice is driving the conclusion. If rankings are stable, exact match is defensible.

---

**Issue 4: The negation/uncertainty entity framing conflates two distinct NLP tasks**

The specific claim: Treating negation cues ('no', 'absent') and uncertainty cues ('possible', 'may represent') as named entity types — and measuring NER F1 on them — tests whether the transformer has superior linguistic understanding of negation scope.

Why this might be wrong: Negation cues and uncertainty cues in radiology reports are, for the most part, closed-class lexical items from a small vocabulary. Their span identification is trivially solved by dictionary lookup (which the CRF explicitly includes as a feature: 'negation/uncertainty keyword presence from a pre-defined list'). What is actually hard about negation in radiology NLP is scope resolution — determining which finding entity a negation cue modifies — which is a relation extraction or dependency-level task, not an NER task. The design tests whether models can identify the token span 'no' or 'possible', not whether they correctly attribute negation to the right finding. Framing this as a test of linguistic understanding is therefore misleading, and the CRF's pre-defined keyword list may essentially solve this sub-task, making it a poor discriminator between models. The design's claim that strong transformer performance on negation cues 'supports a modeling-quality argument' is not warranted by the task as defined.

What would constitute evidence: Report the actual vocabulary coverage of the pre-defined negation/uncertainty keyword list against test set instances. If >90% of negation/uncertainty cue tokens are covered by the list, NER F1 on those entities is near-ceiling for the CRF and provides no information about linguistic modeling quality. Alternatively, include an assertion-level evaluation (is finding X negated?) rather than just cue-span detection.

---

**Summary of verdict reasoning:**

Issues 2 and 4 are structural — Issue 2 introduces an uncontrolled asymmetric information advantage that confounds the primary comparison, and Issue 4 means one of the primary controls against confounds does not actually test what it claims to test. Issue 3 could flip the headline result depending on metric choice. Issue 1 makes the stratified controls statistically uninformative for the sample sizes involved. These are not implementation details; they affect the validity of the primary conclusion.
all_issues_raised: Annotated subset cell sizes under joint modality-department-temporal stratification are too small (~10–30 instances) to support statistically reliable stratified F1 comparisons, undermining the controls against template memorization confounds.

Pre-trained clinical transformers carry an asymmetric pre-training corpus advantage (MIMIC, PubMed) not present in the CRF, which the chronological fine-tuning split does not address and no proposed ablation isolates.

Exact span match F1 may systematically favor or disfavor one model class based on boundary token conventions (subword vs. word-level), not clinical utility, and no lenient-match comparison is included to verify metric robustness.

Negation and uncertainty cues are closed-class lexical items solvable by the CRF's own keyword list, so NER F1 on those entity types does not test scope-level linguistic understanding as claimed, invalidating that control.
all_issues_adjudicated: Annotated subset cell sizes under joint modality-department-temporal stratification are too small (~10–30 instances) to support statistically reliable stratified F1 comparisons, undermining the controls against template memorization confounds.

Pre-trained clinical transformers carry an asymmetric pre-training corpus advantage (MIMIC, PubMed) not present in the CRF, which the chronological fine-tuning split does not address and no proposed ablation isolates.

Exact span match F1 may systematically favor or disfavor one model class based on boundary token conventions (subword vs. word-level), not clinical utility, and no lenient-match comparison is included to verify metric robustness.

Negation and uncertainty cues are closed-class lexical items solvable by the CRF's own keyword list, so NER F1 on those entity types does not test scope-level linguistic understanding as claimed, invalidating that control.
